title: New virus catalog reveals which pathogens pose the greatest threat
gist: A University of Edinburgh team published a catalog of the 239 RNA viruses known to infect humans, arguing that pandemic risk is best predicted by whether a virus already spreads person-to-person and is closely related to established human viruses but emerged separately from animals—the exact profile of SARS-CoV-2—while faster detection is the key lever to deny future pandemics a head start.

insights.claim: Only 239 RNA virus species are known to infect humans out of thousands identified and potentially millions in existence, and the Edinburgh team's catalog ranks which are riskiest.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: A bounded, ranked catalog gives public-health and biosecurity investors a concrete prioritization tool for surveillance and countermeasure spending.
insights.quote: Thousands of RNA virus species have been identified, and there may be millions, but only 239 infect humans.

insights.claim: The greatest pandemic threat comes from viruses that already spread person-to-person, not zoonotic ones; for two-thirds of listed viruses an infected person is highly unlikely to transmit it further.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Reframes where surveillance resources should concentrate—already-transmissible human viruses over rare zoonotic spillovers.
insights.quote: A much greater threat comes from viruses that already have the ability to spread from person to person.

insights.claim: The author contends there is no documented example of an RNA virus acquiring human-to-human spread from a purely zoonotic origin, noting rabies causes tens of thousands of human cases yearly without ever doing so.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: A counter-consensus tempering of bird-flu zoonotic-jump fears; useful for calibrating tail-risk assumptions in pandemic-preparedness bets.
insights.quote: But there is no documented example of an RNA virus doing that. Rabies hasn't, even though there are tens of thousands of human cases every year.

insights.claim: A 2019 finding showed highly transmissible viruses tend to be closely related to other human-spreading viruses but emerge separately from animals—a profile that precisely matched SARS-CoV-2, similar to the original SARS coronavirus but independently acquired from bats.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: This predictive signature is the actionable screen for identifying 'disease X' candidates before they spread.
insights.quote: highly transmissible viruses tend to be closely related to other viruses that spread between humans, but they emerge separately from animals.

insights.claim: The WHO had proposed a SARS-like coronavirus as a disease X candidate the year before COVID emerged, which is why scientists were alarmed from the outset—it matched exactly what they were watching for.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Validates the catalog's predictive value—the framework called the threat category before the pandemic, strengthening the case for pre-emptive surveillance investment.
insights.quote: That's why scientists were alarmed about COVID from the outset—it was exactly what they had been looking for.
insights.locator: The year before, the World Health Organization

insights.claim: Viruses capable of human spread but with an R number too low sustain only limited outbreaks until conditions change—as when Zaire ebolavirus moved from remote villages to a city in west Africa in 2014.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Urbanization can flip a contained pathogen into an epidemic; geographic spread is a leading indicator for escalation risk.
insights.quote: But R numbers can change; for example, when a virus previously confined to remote villages reaches a city.
insights.locator: Then there are viruses that are capable

insights.claim: The short outbreak-virus list—Zaire ebolavirus, Chikungunya, Zika, Oropouche, and mpox—has proven a powerful predictor, with all original entrants going on to cause major epidemics.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: A high-hit-rate watchlist for where the next epidemic-scale event is likely to originate.
insights.quote: Zaire ebolavirus, the insect-borne Chikungunya, Zika and Oropouche viruses, and mpox (a DNA virus) were original entrants, and all have gone on to cause major epidemics.
insights.locator: There have only ever been a few dozen names

insights.claim: Andes hantavirus (behind a recent cruise-ship outbreak) and Bundibugyo ebolavirus (currently spreading in central Africa) lack the profile to start a global pandemic, but a novel measles-related virus would pose a real risk of an emergency worse than COVID.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Directs worst-case planning toward highly transmissible respiratory families like measles rather than current headline outbreaks.
insights.quote: In that scenario, there would be a real possibility of a worldwide emergency much worse than COVID.
insights.locator: By contrast, neither Andes nor Bundibugyo

themes.theme: Detection speed as the decisive lever

insights.claim: Andes, Bundibugyo, and COVID had all been spreading for weeks before detection, so finding and characterizing new viruses faster would deny the next pandemic its head start and materially cut the eventual toll.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Positions rapid pathogen surveillance and sequencing as the highest-leverage investment in pandemic preparedness.
insights.quote: Finding and understanding new viruses faster would deny the next pandemic the same head start, and could make a huge difference to the eventual toll on lives and livelihoods.

insights.claim: Scientists discover only two or three previously unseen human viruses in a typical year, a rate roughly steady since the 1960s, most attracting little attention.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: A low, stable discovery rate means each new find warrants rapid triage against the risk catalog rather than dismissal.
insights.quote: In a typical year, scientists discover two or three viruses that have never been seen in people before.
